title: Comparatives: -er vs more
question: My house is ___ than yours.
explanation: Short adjectives (one syllable) add -er. Big → bigger (double the final consonant).

title: Comparatives: -er vs more
question: This book is ___ than that one.
explanation: Long adjectives (3+ syllables) use 'more' before the adjective. 'Interestinger' is not a word.

title: Comparatives: irregular
question: She is ___ at tennis than me.
explanation: 'Good' has an irregular comparative: good → better. We never say 'gooder' or 'more good'.

title: Comparatives: -er vs more
question: This street is ___ than the one near the park.
explanation: Short adjectives add -er: narrow → narrower. The other forms are not real words.

title: Comparatives: sentence structure
question: The weather today is much ___ than yesterday.
explanation: One-syllable adjectives use -er for comparatives. 'Much' adds emphasis: much colder.

title: Superlatives: -est vs most
question: She is the ___ student in the class.
explanation: One-syllable adjectives use -est for superlatives: smart → smartest.

title: Superlatives: -est vs most
question: That was the ___ film I have ever seen.
explanation: Long adjectives use 'most' for superlatives: boring → most boring.

title: Superlatives: irregular
question: This is the ___ pizza in town.
explanation: 'Good' has an irregular superlative: good → better → best.

title: Superlatives: irregular
question: That was the ___ day of my life.
explanation: 'Bad' has an irregular superlative: bad → worse → worst.

title: Superlatives: sentence structure
question: Mount Everest is the ___ mountain in the world.
explanation: One-syllable adjectives use -est for superlatives: high → highest.

title: First conditional: if clause
question: If it ___ tomorrow, we will stay home.
explanation: In the first conditional, the 'if' clause uses the present simple, not 'will'. If + present simple, will + base verb.

title: First conditional: result clause
question: If you study hard, you ___ the exam.
explanation: In the first conditional, the result clause uses 'will' + base verb: will pass.

title: First conditional: if clause
question: If she ___ early, she will catch the bus.
explanation: The 'if' clause in a first conditional uses present simple: If she leaves...

title: First conditional: negative
question: If you ___ eat breakfast, you will be hungry later.
explanation: In the first conditional, the 'if' clause uses present simple. With 'you', the negative is 'don't' + base verb.

title: First conditional: result clause
question: If we miss the train, we ___ late for the meeting.
explanation: The result clause uses 'will' + base verb: we will be late.

title: First conditional: unless
question: Which word means 'if not' in: '___ you hurry, you will miss the flight.'
explanation: 'Unless' means 'if not'. Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry.

title: First conditional: complete
question: If I ___ enough money, I will buy a new phone.
explanation: The 'if' clause uses present simple. 'If I have' (not 'if I will have').

title: First conditional: result clause
question: If the weather is nice, we ___ to the beach.
explanation: The result clause in a first conditional uses 'will' + base verb: we will go.

title: Adverbs of frequency: position
question: She ___ late for work.
explanation: Adverbs of frequency go after the verb 'be': She is never late.

title: Adverbs of frequency: position
question: I ___ go to the gym on Mondays.
explanation: 'Usually' is an adverb of frequency. It goes before the main verb: I usually go.

title: Adverbs of frequency: position
question: He ___ drinks coffee in the morning.
explanation: 'Always' goes before the main verb: He always drinks. ('Is always' is used with 'be', not with other verbs.)

title: Adverbs of frequency: meaning
question: We ___ eat out. Maybe once or twice a year.
explanation: 'Hardly ever' means almost never. Once or twice a year = very rare.

title: Adverbs of frequency: position
question: They are ___ happy to help.
explanation: With the verb 'be', adverbs of frequency go after it: They are always happy.

title: Adverbs of degree: too
question: This coffee is ___ hot to drink.
explanation: 'Too' + adjective + 'to' means 'more than is good or possible'. Too hot to drink = you cannot drink it.

title: Adverbs of degree: very vs too
question: The film was ___ long, but I still enjoyed it.
explanation: 'Very' is neutral emphasis. Use 'very' before an adjective to intensify it.

title: Adverbs of degree: quite
question: The test was ___ difficult, but most students passed.
explanation: 'Quite' means 'fairly' or 'somewhat'. It goes before the adjective: quite difficult.

title: Adverbs of degree: really
question: I am ___ tired today. I need to sleep more.
explanation: 'Really' is an adverb that means 'very'. 'Too much' is used with nouns/verbs, not with adjectives like 'tired'.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: enjoy
question: I enjoy ___ in the park.
explanation: After 'enjoy', we always use the gerund (-ing form): enjoy walking, enjoy reading.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: want
question: She wants ___ a doctor.
explanation: After 'want', we always use the infinitive (to + verb): want to become.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: avoid
question: You should avoid ___ too much sugar.
explanation: After 'avoid', we always use the gerund (-ing form): avoid eating.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: decide
question: They decided ___ to Paris for the holiday.
explanation: After 'decide', we use the infinitive (to + verb): decided to go.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: finish
question: Have you finished ___ your homework?
explanation: After 'finish', we use the gerund (-ing form): finish doing.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: hope
question: I hope ___ you again soon.
explanation: After 'hope', we use the infinitive (to + verb): hope to see.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: mind
question: Do you mind ___ the window?
explanation: After 'mind', we use the gerund (-ing form): Do you mind opening...?

title: Gerund vs infinitive: learn
question: He is learning ___ the guitar.
explanation: After 'learn', we use the infinitive (to + verb): learning to play.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: suggest
question: She suggested ___ a movie tonight.
explanation: After 'suggest', we use the gerund (-ing form): suggested watching.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: promise
question: He promised ___ me with my project.
explanation: After 'promise', we use the infinitive (to + verb): promised to help.

title: Too and enough: too + adjective
question: He is ___ young to drive a car.
explanation: 'Too' + adjective + 'to' means something is more than the acceptable level. Too young to drive = he cannot drive.

title: Too and enough: adjective + enough
question: She is ___ to ride the roller coaster.
explanation: 'Enough' comes after the adjective: tall enough (not 'enough tall').

title: Too and enough: enough + noun
question: We don't have ___ to finish the project.
explanation: 'Enough' comes before a noun: enough time, enough money.

title: Too and enough: too much
question: There is ___ noise in here. I can't concentrate.
explanation: 'Too much' is used with uncountable nouns. Noise is uncountable, so we say 'too much noise'.

title: Too and enough: too many
question: There are ___ people on the bus. Let's wait for the next one.
explanation: 'Too many' is used with countable plural nouns. People is countable, so we say 'too many people'.

title: Too and enough: not + adjective + enough
question: This bag is ___ for all my books.
explanation: 'Not' + adjective + 'enough': not big enough. 'Enough' always comes after the adjective.

title: Comparatives: as...as
question: Tom is ___ his brother.
explanation: We use 'as + adjective + as' to say two things are equal: as tall as.

title: Comparatives: less
question: This exercise is ___ than the last one.
explanation: 'Less' is the opposite of 'more'. Less difficult = not as difficult.

title: First conditional: question
question: What will you do if you ___ the job?
explanation: In first conditional questions, the 'if' clause uses present simple: if you don't get.

title: Adverbs of degree: enough as adverb
question: He didn't run fast ___ to win the race.
explanation: 'Enough' as an adverb goes after the adjective or adverb: fast enough.

title: Gerund: after preposition
question: She is good ___ languages.
explanation: After an adjective + preposition, we use the gerund: good at learning, interested in doing.

title: Superlatives: one of the
question: Paris is one of ___ cities in Europe.
explanation: 'One of the + superlative + plural noun' is the correct pattern. Long adjectives use 'most'.

title: Too and enough: enough with verb
question: I didn't sleep ___. I'm exhausted.
explanation: 'Enough' can follow a verb to mean 'a sufficient amount': didn't sleep enough.
